description: A17FJ - Day 1: Transposition, Docking and Extraction

tokens: 003:20:04 -And, we're still getting communications through the Apollo Range Instrumented Aircraft.
We'll be picking up through Ascension shortly, at which time we'd expect the communications to improve, the noise to drop off.
From Gene Cernan's report, also from the reports from Ron Evans, it appeared that that Translunar Injection was extremely close to nominal.
The crew read a cut-off time of 5 minutes, 52 seconds.
The premaneuver prediction was 5 minutes, 51 seconds.
And the cut-off velocity appeared to be very, very close to the planned normal.
Booster engineer Frank Van Rensselaer reports the booster cut-off appeared to be exactly normal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 003:21:43 -And Booster engineer now predicting that the maneuver to separation attitude will begin at about 3 hours, 33 minutes, 27 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Houston, how do you read?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I picked it up a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I hope you got all that.
It was a beautiful burn, right through sunrise.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, we copied your VIand your EMS numbers, and we've got a number for you.
Maneuver start time will be at 3 plus 33 plus 27.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: You guys didn't tell us we couldn't see anything going through the sunrise.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're making plans here for a spacecraft Sep time of 03 plus 43.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're copying cabin press of 5.9 this time.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We - we just got it, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: 003:29:42 -This is Apollo Control at 3 hours, 30 minutes.

The Flight Dynamics Officer has just reported that initial tracking, following the Translunar Injection burn, shows the spacecraft to be on a very nominal trajectory, and a relatively small midcourse correction indicated at this time.
The pre-burn prediction on that first midcourse correction was around 5 feet per second and we expect that that will be updated as we get additional tracking following the burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 003:30:28 -In about 3 minutes the spacecraft should - the launch vehicle should be - begin maneuvering to the proper attitude for separation and we're predicting separation to occur at about 3 hours, 43 minutes - or about 13 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Frame 65 for the LMPs mag November November.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: 003:33:28 -About 10 seconds now until the Saturn third stage begins maneuvering into the proper separation attitude.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 003:33:58 -And Booster engineer reports from telemetry data that the booster has begun maneuvering into the proper attitude for spacecraft separation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Now we've got a few very bright particles or fragments or something that go drifting by as we maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's a whole bunce of big ones on my window down there - just bright.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: It looks like the Fourth of July out of Ron's window.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now you can see some of them in shape.
They're very jagged, angular fragments that are tumbling.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They look like fluid of some sort?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: They look like pieces of something.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, for the most part, these fragments are not - or are tumbling at a very slow rate.
I tried a couple pictures of them - different settings.
You may get an idea of what, at least, the patterns look like.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're all ears on these fragments if you think you can figure out what they might be.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Well, you know I - I don't know.
There are a number of possibilities.
If you had some kind of a - I got the impression maybe they were curved a little bit, as if they might be - off the side of the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Or maybe there's paint coming off of it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I noticed on one trip up the elevator last week near one of the flags.
I thought it was on the S-II, but it might have been on the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And the S-IVB maneuver is complete.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And the - with the maneuver complete, the fragment field is essentially static, except for very slight tumbling within the fragments.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Every once in a while, a fragment of considerably higher velocity than the others goes across my window.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, that's that field of view I saw out my window, Jack, do you see it now?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: At least, there - there's no apparent relative motion between fragments.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll take two pictures about a minute apart if I can.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Schmitt's training as a scientist is apparent.
More than most of the other crewmen, he has an inherent tendency to document in order to aid later analysis.
The film record has seven frames that show particles against the black of space.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: My impression is that they are - flat, flake-like particles.
And, although there's no relative motion between the two, most of them seem to be twinkling.
And I think, for the most part, they're all moving away from us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got 0180 and 0 on the old thumbwheels.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: SECS Logic is Closed; SECS Arm are Closed; Logic Power is On.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You have a Go for T&D.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: [Pause.] And we'll hit the GDC Align.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: They are about to begin the Transposition, Docking and Extraction exercise which will begin with separation of the CSM from the rest of the stack and the revealing of the LM on top of the S-IVB as the four panels that make up the SLA come away.
The pyrotechnics that will carry out this dissection of the stack are armed.
The transposition consists of moving the CSM away from the S-IVB, rotating it 180° so that its nose points back towards the stage, then translating back to the LM in order to dock with the lander.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's just [garble] it's kind of diddling.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That's Launch Vehicle Sep, push button.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"As I recall, we undocked and separated just a little earlier than had tentatively been planned, but that was no problem because we were ready to do it."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"As far as the separation from the SLA, it was nominal.
There's a louder bang than I expected from pyros."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Separation of the CSM from the SLA is a fast but complex event.

A set of pyrotechnic devices sever electrical connections between the Service Module and the S-IVB; long lengths of explosive cut the metal structure joining the SM to the SLA to allow the spacecraft to come free; they cut the upper 75% of the conical SLA into four long sections which are now only joined to the S-IVB by spring loaded partial hinges at the centre of their lower edge; pyrotechnic thrusters mounted within the intact portion of the SLA force pistons to push on the outside edge of each SLA panel, causing them to begin rotating away from the enclosed Lunar Module.

Once the panels have rotated about 45° from the centreline of the launch vehicle, the hinges disengage, allowing springs within the hinge assembly to push the panels away at about 2.5 m/s, leaving the LM exposed at the top of the Saturn's third stage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"There seemed to be an awful lot of particles with us continually throughout the flight, both in transearth and translunar coast and in lunar orbit.
These particles were obviously residue from the RCS.
They seemed to be hanging around the LM as a result of pulling in and out of the S-IVB and they were always small particles.
Some, initially, were pieces of Mylar from the S-IVB LM separation.
The others were just like small dump crystals or residue.
On the LM, particularly, when you fire the RCS you could see the RCS residue."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"That residue from the RCS didn't look a lot different than a waste-water dump."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:" That's right, except that it's less dense."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And check the other ones off.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I'm going to start the - My god, look at the junk!
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Houston, we're right in the middle of a snowstorm [laughter].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's going to be bright as all get out.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The scene is captured on the 16mm movie camera.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As the S-IVB and LM come into view, the CSM is still moving away from it.
In the film, the movement can be seen to come to a stop and then the stage begin to grow in the field of view as the CSM approaches.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And there goes one of the SLA panels.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"As soon as we turned around for docking I could see three of the four SLA panels tumbling slowly in space.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"I never did see a SLA panel."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's on the other side of the Earth, if the simulator's any good.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: 003:44:07 -Apollo 17 now in the process of turning around after having separated, blown the pyrotechnic charges that separates the spacecraft from the Saturn third stage.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: There goes another SLA panel, Houston, going the other way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bet you never saw the SLA panels on the simulator.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: No, but we've got the booster and is she pretty.
Challenger's just sitting in her nest.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like Omni Bravo, now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We'll see the - oh, you can't see [garble].
Oh, I can't see my COAS.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, Houston, some of the particles going by the window...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, did you change the DAC?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: ...were obvious enough - fairly obvious to me - paint.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: [Pause.] Okay, did you change the DAP?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston, you want the High Gain?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're standing by for it, and the angles as published on L/3-3 should be good.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Now that the CSM has separated from the S-IVB, Jack can deploy its High Gain Antenna (HGA) which had been folded up beside the SPS engine bell.
This permits a better radio link with Earth with improved bandwidth that will allow good television signals to be sent from the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Ron is in the left seat at the spacecraft's controls.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The High Gain Antenna can be aimed manually at Earth or it will lock on automatically.
Ron has it in Reacq, or Reacquire mode which locks it onto Earth until rotation of the spacecraft causes it to lose lock.
When that happens, it points itself to angles dialled up on two knobs on the right side of the Main Display Console.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We don't have a very good knock - lock on here in Reacq.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're reading you pretty good - voice.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: It dropped off - signal strength dropped off, and now it's picking up again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're getting good signal now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Jack, the High Gain is looking good.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I don't know - about a hundred [garble]...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: It seemed to smoo - slew very smoothly, so it looks all right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can't tell you too much, Bob, from the center seat other than Captain America is very intent on getting Challenger at the moment.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In light of his CSM being namedAmerica, Ron had gained the nickname 'Captain America'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yeah, I'm coming in a little slow, but we've got plenty of time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: 003:49:05 -Ron Evans now at the controls ofAmerica.
Now moving in for the docking with Lunar ModuleChallenger.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"This is the first time that I really noticed that in the plus-X translations, or in any translations as far as that goes, you get about 0.4 degrees per second rates within the dead band.
As opposed to the simulator, it has about 0.1 degrees per second on any of the translations maintaining attitude."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: While we're moving in here, I can see a few chunks of that platy material, possibly paint, down in the SLA sort of bouncing around between the S-IVB and the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But, so far, LM looks very clean.
Can't see anything abnormal from this view yet.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That thing is really stable out there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"Formation flight was great.
The S-IVB by itself was as steady as a rock out there.
I couldn't tell it was dead banding or moving at all.
I came in relatively slow, about 0.1 ft/sec, somewhere in that area."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Can you see it at all, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Got one little chunk coming out - it just came out of the SLA, and it's spinning along a long axis, and it looks very stable.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Every once in a while, a small particle flies off of it though.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How big of a chunk are you talking about, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: How big an item are you talking about?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Oh, I - reference to the thrusters, about the same diameter as the thruster on the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's how long it was, and about - oh, a fifth that thick or that wide.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I don't think - I don't think it's more than a quarter of an inch or maybe even less thick.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That same particle, Bob, came by and as it was spinning, it was throwing off pieces of itself - radially out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This behaviour suggests that the material they are seeing is likely to be ice that is sublimating.
This could have formed on the upper dome of the S-IVB tank, just below the LM.
This tank has contained liquid hydrogen at 20 Kelvin since before launch and although it has insulation on the inside, the outer skin of the tank is still likely to have been extremely cold.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There's small ones come floating by and it looked like flakes.
[Long pause.] And I think I caught three of the four SLA panels going as we were maneuvering.
I've got one out the hatch window now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I saw the fourth one out my side, so we saw them all.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Area around the two spacecraft is cleaned up pretty well by now.
There are just a few fragments moving around.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: 003:54:23 -The crew of Apollo 17 describing what appear to be paint or possibly ice flaking off the Saturn third stage.
But somewhat puzzling at this point is just exactly what the flakes or particles that they're describing might be.
And Apollo 17 in the process of docking with the Lunar Module, preparatory to extracting the LM from the Saturn third stage.
This occuring at some 5,300 nautical miles [9,800 km] from Earth.
And we're watching the spacecraft velocity drop off rapidly as that altitude increases rapidly.
The velocity which at the Translunar Injection cut-off was around 35,000 feet per second [10,600 m/s], down to about 22,000 [6,700 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Rover looks in good shape, so far.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Can you see down on that quad?
Is that what you're looking at?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Yes, I'm looking right at it.
[Pause.] And I got a good view of the MESA top anyway.
It's pretty well covered, but it looks all right also.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The MESA is the Modular Experiment Stowage Assembly.
It's essentially a bay within the LM that is used to stow equipment for the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: All the antennas look good; thruster quads all look great.
I could see all four of them a minute ago.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Stand by for a [garble] on the barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The crew will keep an eye on indicators on Panel 2, the central panel, which show the state of the docking system.
These indicators are found throughout the spacecraft and have a small window which in one state, shows grey, while in the other, a set of black and white diagonal stripes are displayed.
They are reminiscent of the red and white stripes on a traditional barber shop's pole so crews describe their state as either 'barber pole' or 'grey'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The docking system consists of a probe mounted at the tip of the Command Module and a concave conical drogue installed within the docking port at the top of the Lunar Module.
This drogue has a circular hole at its apex and the probe has three captures latches around its tip.
These are arranged to catch on the inside lip of the hole in the drogue.
When they do to, they are said to have captured and this is a soft dock.
The tip of the probe is articulated and damped to accommodate any slight misalignment of the two craft and any rotation one might have with respect to the other.
When such rotations have damped out, the probe is pneumatically retracted while three pitch arms ensure proper alignment of the two craft.
This brings the two faces of the tunnel together where 12 spring-loaded docking latches trip to fasten the two spacecraft as one, the so-called hard dock.
The docking latches engaging in quick succession produce a distinctive ripple-fire sound.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, we're Free; rates look pretty good.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston, ripple fire; but we still have number A barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Evans, from1973 Technical debrief:"Docking was nominal.
As soon as he got capture on the thing, there were no rates.
I didn't have to handle the translation controls or null rates at all.
We went directly to hard dock.
There's more spacecraft movement during that period because I feel that the COAS and the docking target were off a little bit.
And I don't say misaligned, but it's a little bit off.
But, of course, it was in limits and was no problem."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"When we went to retract, we got our big ripple fire - bang on the latches, so we had a relatively good hard dock.
We only got one gray indication on the talkbacks.
The other one was barber pole."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt, from1973 Technical debrief:"There's a lot of descriptive material, I think, in the transcript on that.
As I recall, we got two pulses in the ripple fire.
It seemed like there was one or two latches and then the ripple fire."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"I just recall a woomph!"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt, from1973 Technical debrief:"I think, if you look at the transcript, we said that there were two pulses to it."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we have a Master - and a Master Alarm.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got - we got the most of the latches, but A is barber pole, and B is gray.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, check both circuit breakers; they're In.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We had one clear fire, maybe one or two latches and then a ripple fire on the rest.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, by the way, I had a good view into the AOT, and I can still look in there, and it's very clean.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack seems to be looking out of a CM window, probably window 4 (right rendezvous) and into the objective end of the Alignment Optical Telescope (AOT).
This instrument, mounted at the top of the LM cabin, will be used to align the lander's guidance platform later in the flight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Ron and Gene, we saw your Master Alarm.
Did you have any - anything on the matrix light up?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 003:58:47 -That appeared to be a repeat of the Master Alarm that has been reported several times previously by the crew.

They get the alarm light and tone, but when they look for the exact location of precise indication of what's wrong, it's not there, indicating some sort of a spurious response by the Master Alarm to a problem that doesn't exist.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Bob, we're going to go ahead and take a look at that docking malfunction before we press on here further and check this barber pole out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, We're working some words up here.
We'll be back with you in a second on that, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're down on the checklist through the EDS Power breakers, Open.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Houston, in case we didn't tell you, it's talkback A that's barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Say - say, Gene, we don't think it's a problem.
We'll find out what it is when you get in.
We think we should just press right on with the Flight Plan checklist and keep going.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We just got a Master Alarm when I went to the Retract Prime, from 1 to Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks like panel 2 is jinxed up there, huh?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, O2Heater number 3 went to Auto.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We're reading a Delta-P of greater than 4, and I'm going to open the Pressure Equalization Valve now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Up to this point, the CM forward hatch has sustained a pressure difference equal to the absolute pressure of the CM cabin.
This can be measured by the LM/CM Delta-P meter which can be connected across the two sides of the hatch using the Tunnel Vent Valve.
Now that the LM has been docked onto the tunnel, the meter can indicate the pressure difference between the cabin and the tunnel which at this point will be the same as the CM's absolute pressure.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, the Delta-P is coming down, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The way to bring the Delta-P down; i.e.
to reduce the pressure difference across the forward hatch, is to bleed air from the CM into the tunnel using the Pressure Equalization Valve that is built into the hatch.
At the same time, the Tunnel Vent Valve is set to monitor the Delta-P so the crew can watch its reading fall as the pressure within the tunnel rises.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Gene, while you're watching that, I just thought you'd be interested.
We talked to some of our friends down at the Cape who watched the launch, and they said you were aglow all the way until you faded into - you couldn't tell you from a star.
They saw staging, and they could just see you as a star way off in the distance until you faded out.
Not a cloud in the way at all.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, we're at - we're at 2, and we're monitoring it for 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: While we're checking the integrity here, on mag Alpha Alpha, there's about 50 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Magazine AA is a magazine of 16-mm movie film that is used in a Maurer camera, Also known as a Data Acquisition Camera (DAC).
It was used to film the Transposition & Docking exercise.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 004:11:59 -This is Apollo Control at 4 hours, 12 minutes.
The crew aboard Apollo 17 at this time pressing ahead with their preparations for separating the Lunar Module and Command Module, now docked together, from the Saturn third stage.
You heard some conversation earlier about an indication that all of the docking latches have not locked up.
There are 12 of these latches in the docking mechanism, six of which are instrumented, and of the six that are instrumented, there was an indication that one of those may not have latched.
However, we are confident that more than enough latches have locked up to assure a good solid dock, and for that reason the crew is pressing ahead with their preparations for separation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: It's - Delta-P change is less than 0.1.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 004:13:11 -That report from Gene Cernan indicating that they have a good seal at the docking interface.
Once the hatch is removed between the two vehicles, the crew will get a good look at all of those docking latches and they will be able to tell how many - if any of them didn't latch up.
We're pushing ahead now for - for extracting the Lunar Module, separating from the Saturn third stage at Ground Elapsed Time of 4 hours, 39 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Another reason to gain access to the tunnel area is to connect umbilicals which will allow an electrical signal to be sent from the CM, through the LM to the pyrotechnic devices that will separate the stack from the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, you want cabin's at 4.8 now; Repress is about empty.
No, not yet, it's still getting a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That Repress Package Valve is kind of noisy.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston, the Repress Package is empty now, and we're down to a Delta-P of 0.2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just be advised, you're going to have an S-IVB non-propulsive vent start 04:18:27- You've got about 3 minutes on that.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We seem to be holding Delta-P at about 0.2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You want us to wait until 5 psi for the Emergency Cabin Pressure Selects?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let's just go ahead and just press on.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Let's wait until it gets down a little ways.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Straight up and down as well.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Must be the non-propulsive vent that's banging.
[Laughter.] Here comes all the - look at all the stuff going again.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Your non-propulsive vent gives quite a glow.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That ought to take the surge tank down a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: In order to pressurise the tunnel, air is bled from the CM, reducing the cabin's pressure.
To compensate, oxygen can be supplied from a package in the CM intended for quick repressurisation after, say, a spacewalk.
The package, consisting of three small tanks of oxygen, can then be refilled from the main supply in the SM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: [Pause.] No, they ought to be closed off by now, I think.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Be advised, you don't have to wait until 5 psi cabin to go ahead and open the hatch.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We're pressing on with it now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, it looks like we're going to maintain about 400 on the surge.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Laughter.] I don't know what you're going to do with it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Put it up here in the - in the - on the couch.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, that's a lot lighter than it used to be.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: There's going to be a lot of happy people down there, Bob.
I haven't checked them all, but visually, they're all locked.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me give them a good check.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You'd better check them, because we got a barber pole on that one.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, here's one that didn't come over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What is the position of it?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Maybe we aren't all going to be so happy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, 7, 9, and 10 - the handle is flush; the bungee is vertical, but the handle is not locked down, and the - and the red button is showing.
And I can pull each one of them back slowly.
I haven't done anything with them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The handle is flush; the bungees are vertical, but the handle is not locked down, and the red button is showing on 7, 9, and 10.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Bob, I just pushed the handle on 10 home a little bit and it did lock.
And the red button is flush.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Geno, go ahead and try the handle on 9 and 7; and, if that doesn't work, cock them and refire them starting with 9, please.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: When you trip it with your - did you cock it twice?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And it took two cocks to make it go?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: How about the barber pole now?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Wait a minute, I've got - Docking Probe Main A circuit breaker's In and gone to Retract, and it's gray.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Cocked 7 twice and tripped it, and it's overcenter and locked.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think that takes care of them all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"Subsequent inspection of the latches showed that there were three latches which were not made entirely.
One of them, as I recall, had to be recocked.
Anyway, it turned out that once we got those three latches (which at that time looked like they were operating properly) reset, we got two barber poles on the talkbacks.
Ultimately, latch 4 was found to be unseated on the ring, although, at that time, it looked nominal."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, [pause] Docking Probe circuit breakers are Out and Extend/Retract is Off.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Because it belongs on the probe.
[Garble] it's painted yellow, it belongs on the probe.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Houston, 7-Delta on the test meter is now reading 1.0, it jumped up to 2.6, and is now back to 1.0.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Systems Test Meter allows access to a wider range of spacecraft readings than are allowed by the space available on the cabin panels.
By selecting 7-D on two rotaty switches, Gene can read a voltmeter whose reading from 0V to 5V translates to the power being fed by the CM to the LM.
A chart onpage 1-31of theCSM Systems Checklistindicates how the meter should be interpreted.
Zero volts represents zero amps and 5V is 10A.
Therefore Gene saw the reading go from 5.2A back to 2.0A.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 004:30:06 -This is Apollo Control at 4 hours, 30 minutes.

About nine minutes from now, the crew will be firing the pyrotechnic charges that separate the Lunar Module docked to the Command Module from the Saturn third stage, and springs will push the LM/CSM back away from the launch vehicle at a rate of about 1 foot per second.

And at Ground Elapsed Time of 4 hours, 52 minutes; the launch vehicle will yaw to the proper attitude for an evasive manuever of about 10 feet per second to be performed at a Ground Elapsed Time of about 5 hours, 3 minutes.
This will increase the separation distance to assure no chance of recontact between the booster and the spacecraft en route to the Moon.

On removing the hatch between the LM, the CSM, allowing the crew to get a look inside the docking tunnel, they found that 3 of the 12 latches had not locked up, but on manually recocking them and activating them, they latched up properly, which indicates that there's nothing physically wrong with the system.
And we would expect that the next time the two vehicles come together to dock that the latches will function properly.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: There, we're going up in the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Bob, the hatch is back in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got some new - new angles here for you.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Stand by a minute, and let me find a place to copy them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: What - what kind of angles are they, Bob?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: They're your Noun 22 attitude maneuver for APS burn out of the hatch window.
They're in the middle of the page L/3-5.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I think I'm with you at 3-7; go.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's on 3-5, Jack, middle of the page there.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, I take it back; 3-5, middle of the page.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You notice there's three angles there - 270, make that 274.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And the - no, the next one, the 129-8, change that to 164.
[Pause.] And 4.3 on the yaw, change that to zero.
It's close enough; zero on the yaw.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Roger, and the High Gain angles that you've got on the Flight Plan are close enough and should do it.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: 004:39:13 -Telemetry data now shows the crew loading the information into the spacecraft Digital AutoPilot in preparation for separation from the Saturn third stage.
That should be occurring in the next minute or so.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, I'll - I'll leave it at 10 feet and about an f - f/8.
[Long pause.] Because I had that one to 0180 to 0.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We're aligning our GDC, and the next thing we'll pick up will be the SECS Arm circuit breakers.
And we'll give you a call on the Logic.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: While we're waiting, does the balance on the...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...H2and O2flow in fuel cell 3 - well, actually, in all three fuel cells, look pretty good to you?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, the flows look just right for the current.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Used to seeing them more or less lined up, and I hadn't calculated any farther than that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: O2- O2seems a little higher H2, relatively speaking.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: [Pause.] Verified: SECS Arm breakers are Closed.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We're ready to come up with the Logic.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Houston, Logic 1 is coming on now and Logic 2.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, Houston, just to keep track of EMS null bias check that time, was - went from 100 to 100.7 in 100 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: 17, we'd like to just verify on that top line S-IVB/LM Sep circuit breakers - both of them are Closed?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, we just didn't hear your call and we want to make sure of that.
Didn't want to miss anything here.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: They are - they are verified Closed, and Jack just checked them again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You are Go for Pyro Arm and Go for extraction.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, Go for Pyro Arm; Go for Pyro extraction - or LM extraction [laughter].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, we'll Arm the old Pyros.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Trans Control Power, up and On.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: [Pause.] Okay, I'll wait just a little bit on that - EMS to Normal.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay; on my mark, the S-IVB/LM Sep will come on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, and then I'll back it off to...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, on my mark, S-IVB/LM Sep: 3, 2, 1...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: SECS - SECS Arm breakers are Open.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: America and Challenger are on their own.
LM ejection occurred at 4 hours, 45 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; at an altitude of 13,000 nautical miles [24,000 km] from Earth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"The attitudes given us were excellent; we were able to watch the S-IVB maneuver.
We were able to see the S-IVB vent and it all went well and nominal."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt, from1973 Technical debrief:"It was very clean as far as any debris or anything coming out during the docking phase, and I could see a few little things that were bouncing around inside around the LM, particles of some kind.
It was nothing like previous flights where they had a lot of debris.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now I think we ought to go to the maneuver pretty quick.
Otherwise, the S-IVB will be so far away you can't see it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Pause.] That - that [garble] wasn't as bad as the original Sep.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Mapping Camera and Pan Camera are Off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think we're bowing the right direction.
The Earth is - that's the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The Earth just fills up window 5.
[Pause.] Okay, f, infinity, about a 250th, f/11.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: For those not conversant with photography terms, Ron is setting the focus of the Hasselblad's lens to infinity to photograph Earth.
He will use a shutter speed of 1/250th of a second, a short exposure duration that will minimise any possibility of camera shake blurring the image.
Finally, he will set the lens's aperture (the size of the hole through which light passes on its way to the film) to be 1/11th of the lens's focal length.
Since the lens has a focal length of 80mm, the diameter of the aperture should me about 7.3 millimetres.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next two photographs are the first in the Apollo 17 collection which, although they do not show the whole of Earth's disc, they do allow a measurement to be taken across from limb to limb.
A little analysis then, including a few assumptions, permits us to make an approximate calculation of the spacecraft's altitude when the photo was taken.
In the high resolution scans of this film (mag NN, number 148) made by JSC, the whole height of an imaged frame is 4,175 pixels, measured using one of the earlier shots on the film, AS17-148-22652.
This represents 55.5 mm, the actual height as measured on a negative from a Hasselblad 500C camera, the same type and from the same era as the camera used to take these images.
The image of Earth on frame AS17-148-22701 was measured at 2,522 pixels which implies that the image of Earth on the film itself should have been 33.53 mm across.
On the assumption that the shot was taken using an 80-mm lens, we can use an equation to determine the angle that Earth subtended to the camera.
Since we know Earth's radius to be 6,371 km, basic trigonometry gives us a distance of 31,065 km.
However, that is the distance to the limb, not to the sub-spacecraft point on Earth.
For that we deduct the planet's radius which yields approximately 24,700 km or 13,330 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If we apply the same technique to the next frame, AS17-148-22702, we get a distance of approximately 24,920 km (13,456 nautical miles).
This is because it's image, at 2,503 pixels, is 19 pixels smaller.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There are also a few frames of this Earth view taken on the 16mm movie camera.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yeah, as soon as I find the S-IVB, we'll...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: [Pause.] Looks kind of empty down there without the LM...
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Bob, we're looking right up the dome of the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're standing by for your Go for yaw maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We can give them a Go for - for the yaw, can't we?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You've got the Go for the yaw.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks like she came out of there clean as a whistle.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The yaw maneuver will be starting in about 4 plus 52, a little less than 2 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: At this point, the air-ground audio has a sound like a clockwork mechanism with a tick every one second.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sounds like you are taking a picture of that old dome out there, huh?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: [Laughter.] We're at the end - you know.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The ticking noise returns for about 15 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Look at the aft fire of the thing.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And we can see it firing now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 004:52:54 -The Saturn third stage now maneuvering into attitude for the APS evasive maneuver, a 10-foot-per-second burn using the Auxiliary Propulsion System that will assure...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The old S-IVB had a flare for the dramatic, but it certainly did its job for us.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Again the ticking noise returns but this time it is at 6 ticks per second.
This strongly suggests that what we are hearing is the Maurer 16mm movie camera, also known as the Data Acquisition Camera, which can run at 1, 6, 12 and 24 frames per second.
Magazine AA has footage of the S-IVB making a yaw manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Preliminary data indicated that you are about as nominal as you can be.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: That's the way we'd like to keep it, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: She's - as we're looking at it, she's pitching up.
She was looking right at us - we were looking right at the dome - and now she's pitching up.
The shroud around the IU seems to be totally intact.
It - it looked like a super clean separation.
I can't really see where there's any paint or anything externally chipped off the - the booster from here.
We're beginning to - to pick up the bell.
It's really a shame you don't have this - this whole thing on TV; it's really quite a sight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Mylar and the gold coating on the inside of the shroud that's now visible is also intact.
[Pause.] It looks like you could use it again if you could get it back.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The gold-coloured Mylar thermal insulation is actually for the systems mounted around the inside of the Instrument Unit ring.
As can be seen in AS17-148-22711, neither the remaining section of the conical shroud above it nor the forward skirt of the S-IVB below it carry this insulation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, it's got a job to do when it hits the Moon yet.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: We've - we're almost looking at it broadside now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: She's spitting a little; looks like the yaw maneuver may be complete.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got a - full view of the - entire J-2 from here; and no kidding, Bob, the whole bird, the shroud at the top by the IU, the separation plane down by the S-II, from here all looks as clean as a whistle, all the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you're happy, we'd like a Go from you for the evasive burn.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: You're going to burn on the booster's plus X-axis, is that right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's get a picture or two here yet, and we'll give you a Go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Gene, it'll be about 7 minutes until the evasive burn; 5 plus 03.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: And for your reference, at frame 105 I started a few 250-millimeter pictures of the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Bob, the entire sky, as far as I can make it out through the hatch window, is completely filled with our twinkling flakes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I saw a couple particles go by the window awhile back, and it looked a little bit like insulation in this - these particular case - styrofoam insulation, but in flat flakes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That was right after we separated from the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, I know - I know we're not the first to discover this, but we'd like to confirm, from the crew of America, that the world is round.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Have you gotten a good look at any of that weather down there on the Antarctic?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Well, on Ron's window number 1 - maybe he can tell you a little about it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You know, it's real funny there in Antarctica the - You can see the snow, but there isn't any weather at all in it.
All of the weather's around it in the water.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I don't know what to take a picture of.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Very often, Ron's microphone seems to be open, allowing unusual sounds to be heard on the air/ground circuit.
Earlier, the sound of the DAC movie camera was apparent.
During this pause in Ron's speech, a Hasselblad camera can be heard to operate twice, including the sound of the electric motor drive advancing the film and recocking the shutter.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Look's like you've got a superconservative CMP up there.
We've run off some numbers - Looks like you used about 40 pounds of RCS on the T&D, and you've used about a total of 42 pounds RCS total; so we're hanging right in there.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Very fine; glad to hear that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Still a little bit too much, but that's not bad.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'll be glad to leave all that extra, I hope, in the Service Module when we get home.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's in the Volkswagen pouch down there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Oh, I'll change the lens now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: It's about 30 seconds from the evasive maneuver burn.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: This is a manoeuvre that the APS modules at the base of the S-IVB will carry out to ensure the stage's trajectory does not interfere with that of the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Here, Jack, can you see him good?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: 005:05:11 -This is Apollo Control at 5 hours, 5 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The evasive burn is complete, and the LOX dump will be at 5 plus 24 plus 20.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: He's going to be gone, I think, before we can see it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And, Bob, you can tell Frank to forget the - returning that phone call I made to him a couple days ago.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Think you've had enough booster briefings, huh?
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I figure this is probably the best one of all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Frank said he'd guarantee all those S-IVBs would be just as good as this one.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: The S-IC and the S-II didn't put on a bad show either.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Ron is about to give a frame count of 123 frames on Hasselblad magazine NN.
This implies that frame 122 has been taken and this is the last of four frames that show the whole Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, magazine November November's on about 123 right now.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is the famous Blue Marble sequence, the first time that a human had been in a position to snap a conventional colour photograph of the full Earth.
One of these images is said to be the most reproduced photograph ever.

It is generally reckoned that the third image, 22727, is the definitive image from the series though close inspection of the 2005 scans from the Johnson Space Center suggest to this author (Woods) that 22725 is marginally sharper.
The fact that Ron has just read out the frame number to Earth also suggests that he took the photos.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: By measurement of the size of Earth's image in these photographs (29mm), they were taken at a distance of about 29,400 kilometres (15,900 nautical miles).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Bob, we're on page 3-9 of the Flight Plan now.
We'll check the LM/CM Delta-P, get the cabin fan filter in.
We'll go over the check - the Systems Checklist, get the primary EVAP and a few odds and ends, and start doffing our PGAs.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
